items.title: Add python to local dev env and update python stack
items.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the local Python development story more useful for both product users and maintainers working on proxy-side Python code.

- add a dedicated Python test environment in `Dockerfile.dev` for local proxy-addon work, including `mitmproxy`, `pytest`, and `PyYAML`
- make the shipped Python stack more practical for default-deny sandbox use by documenting Debian `venv` behavior, PEP 668 limits, and the offline `--system-site-packages` workflow
- remove `pipx` from the shipped Python stack and simplify the Python path layout around `~/.local/bin`
- move Go-installed tools to `$HOME/go/bin` and update the Go stack docs and local compose mounts so Go tools do not mask agent binaries such as `codex`
- add common Python local-dev artifacts to `.gitignore`
- refresh README language around supported agents and clarify that public package registries such as PyPI remain opt-in
- normalize the local compose files for this repo's dev environment

items.title: Fix python examples
items.body:
- Fix in loadKeyPair (src/zupt_crypto.cpp): Added code to correct the "has_private" flag in the loaded public key header.
- Fix in example example_basic.py: Converted enc_header from bytes to a list using list(enc_header) before passing to decrypt().
- Fix in SecureBuffer: Added constructor that accepts bytes or a list of integers.
- Fix in to_bytes(): Now returns Python bytes instead of a list.
- Fix in random_bytes, sha256, sha3_512: Now accept bytes as input and return bytes.

NumPy 1.26.4 is a maintenance release that fixes bugs and regressions
discovered after the 1.26.3 release. The Python versions supported by
this release are 3.9-3.12. This is the last planned release in the
1.26.x series.

### [`v1.26.3`](https://redirect.github.com/numpy/numpy/releases/tag/v1.26.3)

[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/numpy/numpy/compare/v1.26.2...v1.26.3)

### NumPy 1.26.3 Release Notes

NumPy 1.26.3 is a maintenance release that fixes bugs and regressions
discovered after the 1.26.2 release. The most notable changes are the
f2py bug fixes. The Python versions supported by this release are
3.9-3.12.

#### Compatibility

`f2py` will no longer accept ambiguous `-m` and `.pyf` CLI combinations.
When more than one `.pyf` file is passed, an error is raised. When both
`-m` and a `.pyf` is passed, a warning is emitted and the `-m` provided
name is ignored.

#### Improvements

`f2py` now handles `common` blocks which have `kind` specifications from
modules. This further expands the usability of intrinsics like
`iso_fortran_env` and `iso_c_binding`.

### NumPy 1.26.2 Release Notes

NumPy 1.26.2 is a maintenance release that fixes bugs and regressions
discovered after the 1.26.1 release. The 1.26.release series is the last
planned minor release series before NumPy 2.0. The Python versions
supported by this release are 3.9-3.12.

items.title: feat: rewrite NTC in C with parallel compilation and cache
items.body:
### Motivation
- Replace the prior Python bootstrap with a native C implementation to satisfy the requirement to make the project entirely C-based and progress toward a GCC replacement. 
- Provide a lightweight, portable driver that can interoperate with host toolchains while offering build caching and parallel compilation. 
- Improve rebuild performance by caching object files keyed by source content, flags, and chosen compiler.

### Description
- Add a single C driver `src/ntc.c` that implements GCC-like option parsing, host toolchain detection (`clang`/`gcc`), per-source compile orchestration, and linking in one binary. 
- Implement content+flags+compiler keyed object caching into `.ntc-cache` and object restoration logic in `src/ntc.c` via a simple FNV-1a hash. 
- Implement parallel per-source compilation using pthread worker threads and task queueing with support for `-j` jobs. 
- Add `Makefile` to build and run tests, and a shell integration test `scripts/test_ntc.sh` that exercises compile-only, link, and cache-hit behavior. 
- Remove the prior Python package/tests and update `README.md` to document the all-C implementation and usage.

### Testing
- Built the project with `make` and ran the integration test via `make test`, which executed `scripts/test_ntc.sh` successfully. 
- Observed the test output showing a successful compile step (`NTC compile succeeded (cache hits: 0)`) and a successful build step with a cache hit (`NTC build succeeded (cache hits: 1)`). 
- The `make clean && make && make test` flow completed without failures on the CI-like environment used for validation.

## Summary by Sourcery

Add validation to ensure Ruff target Python version is consistent with the project’s declared minimum Python version and integrate it into merged config validation, while updating related docs and plans.

Bug Fixes:
- Avoid using Ruff's --isolated flag together with --config when validating merged configurations.

Enhancements:
- Introduce a Python version consistency check between [tool.ruff].target-version and [project].requires-python, optionally failing in strict mode.
- Extend merged configuration validation to run the new version consistency check for pyproject-based configs.
- Clarify uv PATH setup guidance in the contributor documentation.

Documentation:
- Document the Python version mismatch warning, its causes, and remediation steps in the troubleshooting guide.
- Update the ruff-sync usage skill docs to describe the new Python version consistency check and strict mode behavior.
- Mark config validation and Python version consistency items as completed or in progress in the internal planning document.

Tests:
- Add tests covering version mismatch warnings, strict-mode failures, and behavior when validating standalone ruff.toml files.
- Adjust config validation tests to reflect the removal of the --isolated flag when invoking Ruff with --config.

Chores:
- Update the internal agent plan status table to reflect completed and in-progress validation features.

items.title: Drop Python 3.9, add Python 3.14 support
items.body:
This updates the repository’s declared/tested Python support to include 3.14 and aligns the surrounding tooling/docs metadata with that support level. It also adjusts request tests for Python 3.14’s event-loop behavior without changing runtime code.

- **Packaging and support metadata**
  - Add the `Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14` classifier in `pyproject.toml`
  - Extend `.python-version` to include `3.14` so multi-version checks exercise it

- **Docs and tooling alignment**
  - Move docs-related Python pins from `3.13` to `3.14` in:
    - `.readthedocs.yml`
    - `scripts/docs`
    - `scripts/serve`

- **Request test compatibility on 3.14**
  - Keep `pybotters/request.py` unchanged
  - Update `tests/test_request.py` to pass an explicit mock loop when constructing standalone `ClientRequest` instances outside an active event loop

```python
req = ClientRequest(
    method,
    url,
    params=None,
    headers=None,
    data=data,
    auth=None,
    loop=Mock(),
)
```

items.title: docs: Improve documentation quality using copilot
items.body:
This pull request updates the documentation examples for customizing ROOT data reading with uproot-custom, focusing on a clearer workflow for overriding streamers and reading TObjArray branches. The main improvements are a shift to Python-first development for custom readers and factories, step-by-step guidance for porting to C++ for production, and improved clarity and consistency in terminology and code samples.

**Key changes include:**

**1. Python-first workflow for custom readers and factories**
- Rewrites example custom readers (`OverrideStreamerReader`, `TObjArrayReader`) and their factories to use Python as the initial implementation, emphasizing rapid prototyping and ease of use. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL105-R171) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ff64f51f13b722845ff02b6ea5b154b33b1b7880fbb37ae2c4ada18082632b07L212-R263)
- Updates method and class names, and terminology throughout the docs to use "Reader" and "Factory" (capitalized) instead of "reader" and "factory", and clarifies the distinction between Python and C++ implementations. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL4-R6) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL83-R84) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL105-R171) [[4]](diffhunk://#diff-ff64f51f13b722845ff02b6ea5b154b33b1b7880fbb37ae2c4ada18082632b07L5-R14)

**2. Step-by-step porting from Python to C++ for production**
- Adds new documentation sections guiding users to first implement and test custom readers in Python, then port the logic to C++ for better performance, ensuring both versions return the same data structure.
- Provides full C++ code examples and instructions for switching the backend from Python to C++ (`fac.reader_backend = "cpp"`), and explains how to register both Python and C++ readers in the factory. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeR279-R286) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeR295-R387)

**3. API and terminology consistency**
- Renames factory methods (e.g., `build_cpp_reader` → `build_python_reader`) and updates docstrings/comments to reflect the Python-first approach. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL235-R223) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ff64f51f13b722845ff02b6ea5b154b33b1b7880fbb37ae2c4ada18082632b07L355-R362)
- Ensures all references to data structures, method names, and returned values are consistent between Python and C++ examples. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL261-R238) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ff64f51f13b722845ff02b6ea5b154b33b1b7880fbb37ae2c4ada18082632b07L212-R263)

**4. Improved onboarding and development workflow**
- Adds explicit instructions to set the reader backend to Python during development and to switch to C++ for production, making it easier for new users to follow best practices. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeR279-R286) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ff64f51f13b722845ff02b6ea5b154b33b1b7880fbb37ae2c4ada18082632b07R411-R418)

**5. General documentation clarity**
- Cleans up and clarifies code comments, explanations, and step-by-step instructions to make the customization process more accessible and less error-prone for both new and experienced users. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-52fc81db760aa205bb7eb38f6257770408993ba1c88c35a445cfb28421b33baeL4-R6) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ff64f51f13b722845ff02b6ea5b154b33b1b7880fbb37ae2c4ada18082632b07L5-R14)

These updates make the documentation more approachable, encourage rapid prototyping in Python, and provide a clear path to high-performance C++ implementations.

- For any logging statements, is there any chance that they could be logging sensitive data?

items.body:
Ships the Python surface alongside the TypeScript compiler. Same shared IR contract, same Swift output, two host languages.

## What's new

**python/axintai/**
- `ir.py` — Python mirror of the TS IR (cross-language contract)
- `parser.py` — Python DSL parser for @intent-decorated functions
- `sdk.py` — High-level \`defineIntent()\` API, mirrors the TS surface
- `cli.py` — \`python -m axintai compile <file>\` command
- \`__init__.py\` — package exports

**python/tests/** — 17 tests, all green (parser + SDK)

**python/examples/create_event.py** — 28-line quickstart

**python/pyproject.toml** — published on PyPI as \`axintai\` v0.1.0a1 (Python 3.11+, Apache 2.0)

## Why

The moat isn't the compiler — it's \`one definition → every agent surface\`. Python unlocks the huge ML/AI developer community that's more likely to ship Siri integrations than write Swift.

## Scope

- Zero changes to \`src/\` (TypeScript compiler unchanged)
- Self-contained under \`python/\`
- Does not block the other feature PRs

## Tests

\`\`\`
cd python && python -m pytest
# 17 passed
\`\`\`

## Follow-ups (tracked in ROADMAP v0.3.0)

- Formal Intent Manifest spec at spec.axint.ai so TS and Python parsers share a single grammar contract
- Expand Python test suite from 17 → parity with TS (currently 117 TS tests)
- `axintai` → `@axintai/python` namespace alignment post-v1.0

items.title: MAINT: linalg.lstsq: reenable overwrite_x

labels.name: maintenance
labels.description: Items related to regular maintenance tasks

labels.name: C/C++
labels.description: Items related to the internal C/C++ code base

items.body:
#### Reference issue
Towards #24645 by reimplementing `overwrite_a` and `overwrite_b` for `linalg.lstsq`. Can follow up with `qr` as well in the same PR if desired.

#### What does this implement/fix?
Reenables the `overwrite_a` and `overwrite_b` parameters in a similar fashion as was done before for other functions ported to C/C++.

#### Additional information
This is a work-in-progress, posted for early feedback.

- The logic itself should be fine, as evidenced by the added unit tests. (Which might have to be updated to also check for correctness still.)
- However, the computation of the residuals is no longer guaranteed to succeed: it is computed after the fact on the python side, but if `a` and/or `b` are also changed, there is no guarantee that this is correct. For `gelss` and `gelsd` the residuals can be extracted from LAPACK directly, but `gelsy` makes no such promises. In order to get the tests to pass I now added a copy of both `a` and `b` before sending it off to LAPACK, but that should obviously still be removed.

#### Provisory release note
> The behavior of the `residuals` returned by `lstsq` has been changed. For `lapack_driver == "gelsy"` or the system being either underdetermined or square empty residuals are still returned. For `lapack_driver == "gesld"/"gelss"` in combination with an overdetermined system a non-empty residual is always returned. However, in the case where a slice is not full column rank, the corresponding residual is set to `NaN`. 


#### AI Generation Disclosure
No AI

items.title: Fix Railway port handling and trim Docker build context
items.body:
### Motivation
- Railway can inject the runtime `PORT` but shell-style defaults like `${PORT:-8000}` may be passed literally and cause `uvicorn` to fail parsing the port number. 
- Large Docker build contexts (node_modules, notebooks, caches) slow or destabilize remote builds and should be excluded. 

### Description
- Change the Railway start command to run via `sh -c` and bind `uvicorn` to Railway's injected `${PORT}` in `railway.toml`. 
- Update the Docker `CMD` to use `sh -c` and honor `${PORT:-8000}` so the container works both on Railway and locally. 
- Add a `.dockerignore` to exclude `node_modules`, Python caches, test caches, notebooks, logs, and other local artifacts from the Docker build context. 

### Testing
- Ran `python -m py_compile api/main.py` which succeeded. 
- Verified that running `uvicorn api.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port '${PORT:-8000}'` fails with an invalid port error, reproducing the original issue. 
- Verified that starting the server with `PORT=9000 sh -c 'uvicorn api.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT}'` starts successfully, confirming the fix.

items.title: Refactor Python inline scripts to use heredoc syntax
items.body:
## Summary
Refactored Python inline scripts in the CI workflow to use heredoc syntax instead of inline `-c` flag, improving readability and maintainability of shell scripts.

## Key Changes
- **Post validation script**: Converted from `python3 -c "..."` to `python3 - "$post" <<'PYEOF'...PYEOF` syntax
  - Changed variable passing from shell interpolation to `sys.argv[1]`
  - Updated file opening to use the passed argument instead of shell-interpolated string
  
- **SCSS syntax check script**: Converted from `python3 -c "..."` to `python3 - "$f" <<'PYEOF'...PYEOF` syntax
  - Changed variable passing from shell interpolation to `sys.argv[1]`
  - Updated all references from `$f` shell variable to `fname` Python variable
  - Removed unused `re` import

## Implementation Details
- Both scripts now use heredoc syntax with `<<'PYEOF'` delimiter, which improves code readability and eliminates shell escaping complexity
- Variables are now passed as command-line arguments to Python scripts rather than being interpolated by the shell, which is more robust and follows better practices
- The heredoc approach makes the Python code more visible and easier to maintain within the YAML workflow file

items.title: Add Python .gitignore
items.body:
No `.gitignore` existed in the repo, leaving Python build artifacts, caches, and environment files untracked.

## Changes
- Adds standard Python `.gitignore` covering:
  - Bytecode/cache: `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`
  - Build/dist: `build/`, `dist/`, `*.egg-info/`
  - Virtual environments: `.venv/`, `venv/`, `env/`
  - Jupyter: `.ipynb_checkpoints/`
  - Type checkers: `.mypy_cache/`, `.pyre/`, `.pytype/`
  - Env files: `.env`

items.title: Declare free-threaded Python support on the python_function plugin.
items.body:
<!---
Thank you for contributing to NVIDIA DALI! If you haven't yet,
please read the contributing guidelines in the CONTRIBUTING.md file.

We need a few more information from you to proceed.
Please fill the relevant sections in this PR template.

Fields in the Checklist section can be marked after you create and save the Pull Request.
--->


## Category:

**Other** (*e.g. Documentation, Tests, Configuration*)


## Description:

The DALI backend Pybind11 extension already declares support for free-threaded Python. This PR does the same for `python_function_plugin`. This is the missing piece for full free-threaded Python support.

Since `python_function_plugin` is always transitively imported, not declaring free-threaded support results in the GIL always being re-enabled for DALI:
```
frozen importlib._bootstrap>:491: RuntimeWarning: The global interpreter lock (GIL) has been enabled to load module 'nvidia.dali.python_function_plugin', which has not declared that it can run safely without the GIL. To override this behavior and keep the GIL disabled (at your own risk), run with PYTHON_GIL=0 or -Xgil=0.
```
